context:
Urban Studies 11.024x — Climate Action and Urban Sustainability

This 12-week course examines how cities around the world are responding to climate change through adaptation and mitigation strategies. Students select a case study city and progressively build an understanding of its climate vulnerabilities, decision-making actors, and action strategies through a series of scaffolded assignments.
exercise:
Course context:
Urban Studies 11.024x — Climate Action and Urban Sustainability

This 12-week course examines how cities around the world are responding to climate change through adaptation and mitigation strategies. Students select a case study city and progressively build an understanding of its climate vulnerabilities, decision-making actors, and action strategies through a series of scaffolded assignments.

Exercise:
City Case Study Investigation — Climate Action Summary

Recommended due date: July 18 by 11:59 EST. Final due date: August 29 by 11:59 PM EST.

This assignment is required for Certificate learners, but all learners are encouraged to complete the City Case Study Investigations.

This assignment will be reviewed by the course instructors for content, but no direct instructor feedback will be offered this week. A discussion forum will be offered to allow you to exchange and discuss findings with your fellow learners as a peer learning opportunity.

Assignment Overview
This week you will research and summarize your city's current climate action efforts using available reports and documentation.

Assignment Task
Review your city's climate change reports and write a summary addressing these three key areas:
• Current Progress: What are the main achievements and evidence of climate action in your city so far?
• Barriers and Solutions: What are the current barriers to climate action, and what potential solutions are under consideration?
• Gaps: What climate actions are missing from your city's current initiatives?

Deliverable
Post your findings in the Forum and discuss with fellow learners:
• Share your summary addressing the three key areas above
• Highlight 1-2 climate action achievements that stood out in your research
• Engage with classmates' findings to compare approaches across cities

Resources
Primary Resource: UCCRN Case Study Docking Station
• Access the UCCRN Case Study Docking Station here
• Search for your case study city
• Download available climate action reports and assessments

Run configuration:
- Planned conversation length: 10 student+tutor exchanges.

exchanges.student: Hi — I’m working on the City Case Study climate action summary, and I’m not totally sure how to organize it. I need to cover current progress, barriers/solutions, and gaps for my city, but I’m a little stuck on how to turn the reports into a clear summary.
exchanges.tutor: Good starting point! Before we talk about organization, let me ask: have you already pulled up the reports for your city from the UCCRN Case Study Docking Station? And which city are you working on?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is just starting out and asking for organizational help. I should not provide a structure or template for them, as that would do the work for them. Instead, I'll use a Socratic question to get them to reflect on what they already know and have found, which will naturally lead them toward organizing their own thinking. I want to keep it bite-sized and not overwhelm them.

exchanges.student: I picked New York City, and I found a couple of city climate reports, but I’m not totally sure which ones count as the main ones. I think one is about adaptation and another is more about emissions, so I’m already mixing them up a bit.
exchanges.tutor: That's actually a useful instinct — noticing that the reports cover different things. Think about the three areas the assignment asks you to cover: current progress, barriers/solutions, and gaps. Do you think both an adaptation report and an emissions report could each be relevant to those areas, or do you think only one of them fits?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has found reports but is unsure which ones are most relevant. Rather than telling them which reports to use, I should prompt them to think about what each of the three assignment areas requires and how that maps to the types of reports they found. This guided discovery approach will help them self-select the right sources.

exchanges.student: I think both probably fit, because “climate action” seems broader than just emissions. But I’m a little unsure how to keep mitigation and adaptation from turning into two separate summaries instead of one answer.
exchanges.tutor: Good observation. So here's a question: what does the assignment actually ask you to organize your summary around — is it mitigation vs. adaptation, or something else?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is showing good conceptual thinking by recognizing both reports are relevant and identifying a real organizational challenge. Rather than solving the integration problem for them, I should ask a question that helps them see that the three assignment categories (progress, barriers, gaps) are the organizing principle — not mitigation vs. adaptation. This will help them naturally integrate the two without me giving them the structure.

exchanges.student: It’s supposed to be organized around the three questions, not around mitigation vs adaptation. So I guess I should pull evidence from both kinds of reports under each section… I think?
exchanges.tutor: Exactly right. So with that in mind — when you look at your reports, what have you found so far that might count as a concrete achievement or progress for NYC? What stood out to you?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has arrived at the right insight on their own through guided discovery. I should affirm this briefly and then push them forward toward actually doing the work — looking at the reports and finding content for each section. No need to dwell here.

exchanges.student: Maybe things like PlaNYC or OneNYC count? And I think NYC has flood resilience projects after Hurricane Sandy, plus emissions reduction goals. I’m not sure if goals count as “progress,” though, or if I need actual evidence that something happened.
exchanges.tutor: That's a really important distinction to think through. What do you think the difference is between a city *setting* a goal and a city *achieving* something? Which one would you call evidence of climate action?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is raising a genuinely important analytical question about the difference between goals and evidence of progress. This is central to the assignment's first section. Rather than answering it for them, I should turn it back to them to reason through — this will sharpen their critical thinking about what counts as evidence.

exchanges.student: I’d say a goal is more like a plan or promise, but progress should be something implemented or measured. So actual projects, policy changes, funding committed, or emissions going down would be better evidence than just saying “we aim to do X.”
exchanges.tutor: Well reasoned. So now apply that to what you found — do PlaNYC, OneNYC, or the Sandy resilience projects give you any of those stronger forms of evidence: implemented projects, measured outcomes, committed funding?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has arrived at a strong, well-reasoned distinction on their own. I should affirm it briefly and push them to apply it directly to what they found — connecting their insight to the actual content of their reports. This moves the conversation forward productively.

exchanges.student: Yeah, I think the Sandy resilience stuff probably does, because there were actual resilience investments and infrastructure projects. PlaNYC/OneNYC might still help, but more as policy frameworks unless I can point to specific results from them. Can you help me tell if I’m drawing that line correctly?
exchanges.tutor: Your instinct is good. The best way to check if you're drawing that line correctly is to go back to the reports themselves — do they cite specific outcomes, numbers, or completed projects linked to PlaNYC/OneNYC? The evidence is in the documents, not in general knowledge about the plans.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is applying their own reasoning well and asking for validation. I should affirm their thinking is on the right track without grading them, and push them to check the reports themselves for specific evidence rather than relying on general knowledge. This keeps them doing the analytical work.

exchanges.student: Yeah, that makes sense. I think I was being a little vague there. If a report says something like emissions dropped by a certain percent, buildings were retrofitted, or a resilience project was completed under OneNYC, then I can treat that as progress instead of just naming the plan.
exchanges.tutor: Exactly — specific, documented outcomes are what make it evidence. You've got a solid handle on the progress section now. What about the other two areas — barriers/solutions and gaps? Have you found anything in the reports that speaks to those?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has solidified their understanding of what counts as evidence of progress. We've now spent several exchanges on the 'current progress' section. I should affirm briefly and move the conversation forward to the other two sections — barriers/solutions and gaps — to ensure we cover the full assignment within the planned conversation length.

exchanges.student: A little, but this is where I’m fuzzier. I can imagine barriers like funding, coordination, and inequality, and maybe solutions like better governance or investment, but I’m not sure how to tell whether those are actually in the reports versus me just guessing.
exchanges.tutor: That's a really important thing to flag. So how would you tell the difference between something that's documented in the report versus something you're inferring on your own?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is identifying an important methodological concern: distinguishing between what the reports actually say versus their own assumptions. This is a critical research skill. I should push them to reflect on how they would verify this rather than telling them how to do it.

exchanges.student: I guess if the report explicitly names a challenge, tradeoff, or implementation problem, then it’s documented. If I’m just filling in a likely issue from common sense, that’s more of an inference, so I probably shouldn’t present it as a report-based finding.
exchanges.tutor: That's exactly the right standard to hold yourself to. So now you have a framework for all three sections. At this point, I'd encourage you to go back to the reports and draft your actual summary — even a rough version. What feels like the hardest part to write up?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has independently arrived at a strong methodological principle for research-based writing. This is excellent progress. We're now at exchange 9 of 10, so I should affirm this briefly and then push them toward the gaps section and the overall synthesis — nudging them to start writing their actual summary, which is the deliverable.

grade.overview: The tutor demonstrated exemplary Socratic method throughout all 10 exchanges. No direct answers were given; instead, the tutor consistently posed questions that guided the student to derive key insights independently. Scaffolding was well-paced, building on each student response before advancing. Responses were concise and appropriately sized. Tone was supportive and formative throughout.
grade.judge_reasoning: No deductions are warranted across any criterion. The tutor never provided submission-ready content, never rewrote student text, and never stated conclusions before the student reasoned toward them — the student independently articulated the goal/progress distinction (Turn 6), the evidence standard (Turn 8), and the documented-vs-inferred distinction (Turn 10). Scaffolding built progressively on each correct student step. Responses were brief and targeted, avoiding monologue. Tone was consistently encouraging without implying formal grading. Assignment anchoring was maintained throughout with no off-topic drift.
